title: Watts to Amps Calculator
tagline: Convert watts to amps from power, voltage, and optional power factor for DC loads and single-value AC estimates.
intro: Enter power and voltage to calculate current. Add a power factor when you want an AC estimate instead of the default resistive or DC case.

error.invalid: Please enter valid positive numbers for power and voltage, and use a power factor between 0 and 1.

hint: Leave power factor blank to use the default value of 1.0, which matches DC circuits and many purely resistive loads.

formula.dc: For DC or resistive loads: I = P / V
formula.ac: For AC loads with power factor: I = P / (V × PF)

about.heading: About Watts to Amps Conversion
about.body:
A watts to amps calculator converts electrical power into current draw by combining the power rating with the operating voltage. In the simplest DC case, the relationship is I = P / V. If a load consumes 500 watts at 12 volts, the current is 41.67 amps. This is one of the most useful quick calculations in electronics, automotive systems, solar installations, marine wiring, and appliance sizing because current determines conductor size, fuse selection, breaker limits, and voltage-drop concerns.

The reason the formula works is that electrical power equals voltage multiplied by current: P = V × I. Rearranging for current gives I = P / V. That is the correct relationship for DC circuits and for AC loads that behave like purely resistive devices. In real AC systems, however, not all of the apparent power becomes real power. Some of it can be tied up in the phase shift between voltage and current caused by inductive or capacitive components. That is where power factor comes in.

Power factor, usually written as PF, measures how effectively current is being converted into useful real power in an AC circuit. A power factor of 1 means voltage and current are perfectly in phase, so the DC-style formula works directly. Lower power factors mean the circuit must draw more current to deliver the same number of watts. For single-value estimates, current becomes I = P / (V × PF). This is why a 1,200-watt load at 120 volts and a 0.95 power factor draws a bit more current than the same 1,200 watts at PF = 1. The difference matters when sizing branch circuits, inverters, generators, and protective devices.

This calculator is especially useful when you know the power rating but need to understand installation impact. A 2,000-watt heater on a 240-volt supply draws 8.33 amps if it is essentially resistive. A 500-watt 12-volt DC load draws 41.67 amps, which immediately tells you the wiring and fuse requirements will be much more demanding than the raw wattage alone might suggest. Household appliances, RV systems, battery banks, and workshop tools all become easier to compare once you express them in amps.

Like any simplified calculator, this tool is best used as a practical estimate. Three-phase systems, motor starting currents, harmonic distortion, and detailed apparent-power calculations need more specialized treatment. Still, for routine design checks, educational examples, and everyday troubleshooting, converting watts to amps with voltage and optional power factor gives a fast, informative picture of real current demand. It helps translate a nameplate wattage into the quantity that most directly drives safe electrical sizing.

examples.heading: Examples
examples.intro: These examples show how current changes with voltage level and how power factor affects AC current draw.

items.note: A near-unity AC load still draws slightly more current than the pure DC or resistive case because PF is below 1.
This is the straightforward resistive or DC-style relationship where current equals watts divided by volts.
Low-voltage systems require high current for the same wattage, which is why cable sizing becomes critical in battery-based setups.
A lower AC power factor raises current and can materially affect breaker and conductor selection.

howto.heading: How to Use
steps: Enter the real power in watts and the operating voltage in volts. Both fields are required for every calculation.
Leave power factor blank for DC or purely resistive loads, or enter a value between 0 and 1 for an AC estimate.
Click Calculate to see the current in amps and the formula used for the scenario.
Use the worked examples to compare household AC loads, resistive heaters, and low-voltage DC systems, then reset the form for a new case.

items.q: Why does lower voltage mean higher current for the same power?
items.a: Power is the product of voltage and current, so if the wattage stays fixed and voltage goes down, current must go up. That is why low-voltage systems often need thicker conductors and larger protective devices.

Power factor measures how effectively AC current is turned into useful real power. A power factor of 1 means ideal alignment between voltage and current, while lower values indicate more current is needed to deliver the same watts.

items.q: Should I leave power factor blank for DC circuits?
items.a: Yes. DC circuits do not use AC phase relationships, so the effective power factor is treated as 1 and the formula simplifies to current equals power divided by voltage.

items.q: Can I use this for motors and compressors?
items.a: You can use it for a steady-state estimate if you know the running watts and an approximate power factor. However, motors may have startup surge current and operating characteristics that require more detailed analysis.

items.q: Why is this useful for electrical sizing?
items.a: Circuit protection and conductor sizing are based on current, not just on wattage. Converting watts to amps helps you assess breaker loading, fuse selection, voltage drop, and cable requirements before installation.
